title: Asthma — Acute Severe
oneLiner: Acute worsening of asthma with PEF 33–50% predicted; requires immediate recognition to avoid progression to life-threatening respiratory failure.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax:** sudden unilateral chest pain, deviated trachea, hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, hypotension → needle decompression before chest X-ray.
- **Silent chest (no wheeze):** failing respiratory effort – the patient is too breathless to wheeze. PEF <33%, SpO2 <92%, exhaustion, rising PaCO2. This is a pre-arrest state; call ICU / anaesthetics immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** progressive dyspnoea, cough, wheeze, chest tightness – but distinguish from choking/fix/angina.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Respiratory rate >25/min
  - Tachycardia >110/min (sinus)
  - Inability to complete sentences (single words only)
  - Use of accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, subcostal recession)
  - PEFR: 33–50% of best/predicted
  - SpO2 <92% on room air (if life-threatening, <92% on O2)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Acute severe asthma exacerbation (wheeze, hyperinflation, previous diagnosis)
- **Less likely but important:**
  - Exacerbation of COPD (older, smoking history, less reversibility, chronic productive cough)
  - Foreign body aspiration (acute onset, unilateral wheeze, choking history, young child/elderly)
  - Anaphylaxis (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension, acute onset after allergen)
  - Pulmonary embolism (pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, acute hypoxia with clear chest, raised D-dimer/Wells)
- **Splitter:** Check if wheeze is universal or unilateral. Unilateral wheeze → foreign body or effusion, not asthma.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - PEFR (pre- and post- initial bronchodilator) – document % predicted/best
  - SpO2 continuous
  - ABG if SpO2 <92% on high-flow O2, or if exhaustion/silent chest – look for normal/high PaCO2 (bad), low pH, low PaO2
  - Chest X-ray? Only if suspicion of pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, or consolidation (routine not needed)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (eosinophilia suggests asthma, neutrophilia suggests infection)
  - CRP (raised in infection, but often normal)
  - U&Es (potassium can drop with β-agonists)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR first-line (PA + lateral if possible, but often port barrel)
  - CT chest only if non-resolving or suspected interstitial lung disease/vasculitis (not acute)
- **Special tests:** Sputum culture if productive cough. IgE and specific allergens for chronic management (not acute).
- **Never-miss test:** Arterial blood gas (ABG) in any patient with SpO2 <92% on O2, or clinical life-threatening features. A normal PaCO2 in acute asthma is not reassuring – it may indicate impending exhaustion. Rising PaCO2 is a medical emergency.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - High-flow oxygen to target SpO2 94–98% (not 100% if risk of CO2 retention? In asthma, O2 is safe – target 94-98%)
  - Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (or via pMDI + spacer if mild-mod, but severe usually neb)
  - Add ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg to first neb if severe (PEF <50%)
  - Prednisolone 40-50 mg PO (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg if vomiting/very unwell)
  - Repeat salbutamol nebs every 20-30 mins if poor initial response – max 3 doses in first hour
  - Consider IV magnesium sulphate 2 g over 20 min (single dose) if life-threatening features or poor response to initial therapy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue salbutamol nebs 4-6 hourly (or more frequent as clinically needed)
  - Prednisolone 40-50 mg OD for 5-7 days (wean not needed)
  - Monitor PEFR pre- and post-neb – should improve >75% predicted by discharge
  - Check inhaler technique, step-up preventer therapy (e.g. add LABA/ICS, or increase ICS – discharge plan)
  - If not improving, consider IV salbutamol or IV aminophylline (ICU setting – [check local policy])
  - Ensure chest physiotherapy if mucus plugging suspected
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to asthma specialist nurse for personalised action plan (PAP)
  - Optimise inhaled therapy: step-up based on BTS/SIGN guidelines (e.g. low-dose ICS + LABA, medium-dose ICS)
  - Consider FeNO testing, spirometry, atopy assessment
  - Smoking cessation, allergy avoidance
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance rate if nil by mouth or dehydrated (but be careful in severe asthma, avoid fluid overload – e.g. 500 mL bolus only if hypovolaemic then reassess)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless contraindicated (e.g. active bleed)
  - Diet/NBM: NBM only if severe distress; otherwise normal diet.
  - Observation frequency: every 15 minutes until stable then hourly – record RR, SpO2, PEFR, pulse, sentence ability.
  - Ensure written asthma plan – step-up and step-down instructions.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Salbutamol 5 mg (nebulised) – can repeat every 20–30 min in acute severe (up to 3 back-to-back in first hour). If life-threatening, consider continuous nebulisation (10 mg/h) [check local policy].
  - Ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg (nebulised) – add to first neb, then every 6 hours if required.
  - Prednisolone 40–50 mg PO OD for 5–7 days. No taper needed.
  - Hydrocortisone IV 200 mg (if unable to swallow or life-threatening) then switch to prednisolone.
  - Magnesium sulphate IV 2 g over 20 min – once, not repeated.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - IV salbutamol bolus 250 mcg over 5 minutes (severe/life-threatening, then infusion [check local policy]).
  - IV aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading over 20 min (if not on oral theophylline) then 0.5–0.7 mg/kg/hr – requires monitoring, toxicity risk.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - β-blockers (including eye drops) – avoid.
  - NSAIDs – may provoke bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive asthmatics.
  - Theophylline – interactes with ciprofloxacin, macrolides, cimetidine → toxicity.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for standard asthma drugs. Salbutamol, prednisolone – no adjustment. Aminophylline – hepatically cleared; reduce dose in liver disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Overuse of IV aminophylline without EKG and theophylline level – can cause seizures or fatal arrhythmia. Always check if patient already taking oral theophylline. **Never give a loading dose to a patient on maintenance theophylline** – check drug chart.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory arrest / need for intubation and mechanical ventilation
  - Pneumothorax (especially with vigorous bagging)
  - Pneumomediastinum
  - Lactic acidosis from excessive β-agonist use (may mimic worsening)
  - Hypokalaemia (from β-agonists)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (rare, with high-dose β-agonists or aminophylline)
- **Chronic:**
  - Fixed airflow obstruction (incomplete reversibility)
  - Side effects of long-term high-dose ICS: osteoporosis, cataract, adrenal suppression
  - Bronchiectasis (if recurrent severe exacerbations with mucus plugging)
sections.Prognosis: - Most acute severe asthma episodes respond within 2–3 days to systemic steroids and bronchodilators. Risk of death is low (0.3–0.5/100,000/yr in UK) but near-fatal asthma carries significant morbidity. In ICU survivors, long-term lung function and quality of life may be impaired.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do a quick “sentence test” on arrival** – if they cannot complete a sentence, they are at least acute severe.
- **Always give systemic steroids within 1 hour of presentation** – reduces admission rates and mortality.
- **Check ABG early in those not responding** – a normal PaCO2 is a warning sign, not a good sign.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on oxygen saturation alone** – a patient on high-flow O2 may have a high SpO2 but still be tiring (rising PaCO2).
- **Do not give routine antibiotics** – infection is not the cause in most acute severe asthma (viral triggers are common). Antibiotics only indicated if clear consolidation or purulent sputum.
- **Do not discharge until PEFR >75% predicted and stable** – early discharge without plan leads to re-admission.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness despite increased bronchodilator use, inability to speak sentences, no improvement after 3 puffs of salbutamol via spacer every 20 min for 2 hours, or if peak flow drops below 50% personal best.
- **Follow-up:** Asthma nurse clinic within 48h of discharge, review by GP within 1 week.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior/ICU:** Life-threatening features (silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion, SpO2 <92% despite O2, PEF <33%, rising PaCO2, low GCS, arrhythmia, hypotension). Also if <30% improvement after initial treatment.
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥ 7 (or rising), SpO2 < 92% on 15L O2, respiratory rate >30/min, heart rate >130/min, GCS <15, systolic BP <90 mmHg. Any single life-threatening feature → immediate anaesthetics/Call for help.

rawMarkdown:
## Asthma — Acute Severe
**One-liner:** Acute worsening of asthma with PEF 33–50% predicted; requires immediate recognition to avoid progression to life-threatening respiratory failure.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax:** sudden unilateral chest pain, deviated trachea, hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, hypotension → needle decompression before chest X-ray.
- **Silent chest (no wheeze):** failing respiratory effort – the patient is too breathless to wheeze. PEF <33%, SpO2 <92%, exhaustion, rising PaCO2. This is a pre-arrest state; call ICU / anaesthetics immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** progressive dyspnoea, cough, wheeze, chest tightness – but distinguish from choking/fix/angina.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Respiratory rate >25/min
  - Tachycardia >110/min (sinus)
  - Inability to complete sentences (single words only)
  - Use of accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, subcostal recession)
  - PEFR: 33–50% of best/predicted
  - SpO2 <92% on room air (if life-threatening, <92% on O2)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Acute severe asthma exacerbation (wheeze, hyperinflation, previous diagnosis)
- **Less likely but important:**
  - Exacerbation of COPD (older, smoking history, less reversibility, chronic productive cough)
  - Foreign body aspiration (acute onset, unilateral wheeze, choking history, young child/elderly)
  - Anaphylaxis (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension, acute onset after allergen)
  - Pulmonary embolism (pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, acute hypoxia with clear chest, raised D-dimer/Wells)
- **Splitter:** Check if wheeze is universal or unilateral. Unilateral wheeze → foreign body or effusion, not asthma.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - PEFR (pre- and post- initial bronchodilator) – document % predicted/best
  - SpO2 continuous
  - ABG if SpO2 <92% on high-flow O2, or if exhaustion/silent chest – look for normal/high PaCO2 (bad), low pH, low PaO2
  - Chest X-ray? Only if suspicion of pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, or consolidation (routine not needed)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (eosinophilia suggests asthma, neutrophilia suggests infection)
  - CRP (raised in infection, but often normal)
  - U&Es (potassium can drop with β-agonists)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR first-line (PA + lateral if possible, but often port barrel)
  - CT chest only if non-resolving or suspected interstitial lung disease/vasculitis (not acute)
- **Special tests:** Sputum culture if productive cough. IgE and specific allergens for chronic management (not acute).
- **Never-miss test:** Arterial blood gas (ABG) in any patient with SpO2 <92% on O2, or clinical life-threatening features. A normal PaCO2 in acute asthma is not reassuring – it may indicate impending exhaustion. Rising PaCO2 is a medical emergency.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - High-flow oxygen to target SpO2 94–98% (not 100% if risk of CO2 retention? In asthma, O2 is safe – target 94-98%)
  - Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (or via pMDI + spacer if mild-mod, but severe usually neb)
  - Add ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg to first neb if severe (PEF <50%)
  - Prednisolone 40-50 mg PO (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg if vomiting/very unwell)
  - Repeat salbutamol nebs every 20-30 mins if poor initial response – max 3 doses in first hour
  - Consider IV magnesium sulphate 2 g over 20 min (single dose) if life-threatening features or poor response to initial therapy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue salbutamol nebs 4-6 hourly (or more frequent as clinically needed)
  - Prednisolone 40-50 mg OD for 5-7 days (wean not needed)
  - Monitor PEFR pre- and post-neb – should improve >75% predicted by discharge
  - Check inhaler technique, step-up preventer therapy (e.g. add LABA/ICS, or increase ICS – discharge plan)
  - If not improving, consider IV salbutamol or IV aminophylline (ICU setting – [check local policy])
  - Ensure chest physiotherapy if mucus plugging suspected
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to asthma specialist nurse for personalised action plan (PAP)
  - Optimise inhaled therapy: step-up based on BTS/SIGN guidelines (e.g. low-dose ICS + LABA, medium-dose ICS)
  - Consider FeNO testing, spirometry, atopy assessment
  - Smoking cessation, allergy avoidance
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance rate if nil by mouth or dehydrated (but be careful in severe asthma, avoid fluid overload – e.g. 500 mL bolus only if hypovolaemic then reassess)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless contraindicated (e.g. active bleed)
  - Diet/NBM: NBM only if severe distress; otherwise normal diet.
  - Observation frequency: every 15 minutes until stable then hourly – record RR, SpO2, PEFR, pulse, sentence ability.
  - Ensure written asthma plan – step-up and step-down instructions.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Salbutamol 5 mg (nebulised) – can repeat every 20–30 min in acute severe (up to 3 back-to-back in first hour). If life-threatening, consider continuous nebulisation (10 mg/h) [check local policy].
  - Ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg (nebulised) – add to first neb, then every 6 hours if required.
  - Prednisolone 40–50 mg PO OD for 5–7 days. No taper needed.
  - Hydrocortisone IV 200 mg (if unable to swallow or life-threatening) then switch to prednisolone.
  - Magnesium sulphate IV 2 g over 20 min – once, not repeated.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - IV salbutamol bolus 250 mcg over 5 minutes (severe/life-threatening, then infusion [check local policy]).
  - IV aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading over 20 min (if not on oral theophylline) then 0.5–0.7 mg/kg/hr – requires monitoring, toxicity risk.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - β-blockers (including eye drops) – avoid.
  - NSAIDs – may provoke bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive asthmatics.
  - Theophylline – interactes with ciprofloxacin, macrolides, cimetidine → toxicity.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for standard asthma drugs. Salbutamol, prednisolone – no adjustment. Aminophylline – hepatically cleared; reduce dose in liver disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Overuse of IV aminophylline without EKG and theophylline level – can cause seizures or fatal arrhythmia. Always check if patient already taking oral theophylline. **Never give a loading dose to a patient on maintenance theophylline** – check drug chart.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory arrest / need for intubation and mechanical ventilation
  - Pneumothorax (especially with vigorous bagging)
  - Pneumomediastinum
  - Lactic acidosis from excessive β-agonist use (may mimic worsening)
  - Hypokalaemia (from β-agonists)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (rare, with high-dose β-agonists or aminophylline)
- **Chronic:**
  - Fixed airflow obstruction (incomplete reversibility)
  - Side effects of long-term high-dose ICS: osteoporosis, cataract, adrenal suppression
  - Bronchiectasis (if recurrent severe exacerbations with mucus plugging)

### Prognosis
- Most acute severe asthma episodes respond within 2–3 days to systemic steroids and bronchodilators. Risk of death is low (0.3–0.5/100,000/yr in UK) but near-fatal asthma carries significant morbidity. In ICU survivors, long-term lung function and quality of life may be impaired.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do a quick “sentence test” on arrival** – if they cannot complete a sentence, they are at least acute severe.
- **Always give systemic steroids within 1 hour of presentation** – reduces admission rates and mortality.
- **Check ABG early in those not responding** – a normal PaCO2 is a warning sign, not a good sign.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on oxygen saturation alone** – a patient on high-flow O2 may have a high SpO2 but still be tiring (rising PaCO2).
- **Do not give routine antibiotics** – infection is not the cause in most acute severe asthma (viral triggers are common). Antibiotics only indicated if clear consolidation or purulent sputum.
- **Do not discharge until PEFR >75% predicted and stable** – early discharge without plan leads to re-admission.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness despite increased bronchodilator use, inability to speak sentences, no improvement after 3 puffs of salbutamol via spacer every 20 min for 2 hours, or if peak flow drops below 50% personal best.
- **Follow-up:** Asthma nurse clinic within 48h of discharge, review by GP within 1 week.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior/ICU:** Life-threatening features (silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion, SpO2 <92% despite O2, PEF <33%, rising PaCO2, low GCS, arrhythmia, hypotension). Also if <30% improvement after initial treatment.
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥ 7 (or rising), SpO2 < 92% on 15L O2, respiratory rate >30/min, heart rate >130/min, GCS <15, systolic BP <90 mmHg. Any single life-threatening feature → immediate anaesthetics/Call for help.
